data.title: S&P 500 Recovers As Risk Of 2026 Rate Hikes Falls
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPX) recovered during the Good Friday holiday-shortened trading week. The index closed at 6,582.69 on Thursday, 2 April 2026, up nearly 3.4% from its previous trading week's close.

Given that the week featured news of higher oil and fuel prices, which has been a catalyst for falling stock prices in recent weeks, that outcome seems paradoxical. When you add a surprisingly strong jobs report into the mix, it would seem certain the Federal Reserve would be more likely to hike interest rates in upcoming months. After all, the fear of the Fed raising interest rates to fight inflation from the Iran war surge of oil prices has been front and center in financial news headlines in shaping investor expectations of the future.

But the week saw something different with the actual expectations for how the Fed would be setting interest rates through the end of 2026. The CME Group's FedWatch Tool foresees no interest rate changes through the end of 2026, with a much lower probability of any rate hike taking place. The FedWatch tool even sees a growing chance for a quarter-point rate cut in 2027, with the most likely timing of that change coming near the end of 2027-Q3.

What's going on is that the oil price surge is now raising the potential of a recessionary contraction in the U.S. economy, which increased after President Trump's mid-week speech boosted the prospect the Iran war would intensify in the near term rather than come to a resolution as had previously been expected. The heightened risk of contraction would forestall the Fed from hiking rates in 2026 and would support the S&P 500 holding onto its gains during the week in the absence of additional information for how that would affect the various parts of the U.S. economy. The latest update of the alternative futures chart shows the rebound:

As the geopolitical event progresses, we're likely to continue seeing an elaborate dance between investor expectations for inflationary pressures related to the disruption of oil markets, interest rates, and the outlook for businesses as the economy copes with all of it. The market-moving headlines of the week that was give a taste of those dynamics:

Monday, 30 March 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil passed $100 a barrel, with prices expected to keep rising
      • Airlines face fare dilemma as fuel spike threatens travel demand
  • Chief Fed official says Fed will stand still on interest rates, Number Two official says Fed's policy can handle 'unusual' circumstances:
    • Powell says Fed can 'wait and see' how war affects inflation
      • Fed watching private credit sector for signs of trouble, Powell says
    • Fed's Williams says monetary policy 'well positioned' for 'unusual' circumstances
    • Fed's faith in anchored inflation expectations may be coming under stress
  • Growth signs developing in China:
    • China's factory activity seen returning to expansion in March: Reuters poll
  • BOJ officials worried about effect of oil and their own monetary policies on Japan's inflation:
    • BOJ highlights inflationary pressure from oil, weak yen
  • Bigger trouble developing in the Eurozone:
    • German inflation spikes to 2.8% in March as energy costs soar
  • ECB officials starting to think they may not have the perfect monetary policy they thought:
    • ECB must respond quickly to signs of inflation drift, says Stournaras
    • Villeroy says ECB ready to act, but too early to discuss timing of any rate hike
  • Wall Street's major averages ended mostly lower to kick off the holiday-shortened week
    • S&P 500 is on pace for its worst month since 2022 as broad selloff deepens
    • Wall Street indexes mostly fall as Iran war widens

Tuesday, 31 March 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil Falls on Signs From US, Iran of Openness for War Resolution
      • Iran reiterates willingness to end war but needs guarantees- reports
    • WTI, June Brent crude futures slide on report that Iran may be ready to end war
      • US pump prices hit $4 a gallon
    • Private lenders delay reckoning with payment concessions on stressed debt
    • US single-family home prices rise moderately in January, FHFA says
  • Fed official warns against letting down guard against inflation:
    • Fed's Schmid warns against complacency on inflation
  • Bigger trouble, stimulus developing in China:
    • China set to extend fuel export ban with small exemptions, sources say
      • China's smaller independent refiners to curb output on rising oil prices, sources say
    • China to maintain appropriately loose monetary policy
  • Bigger inflation developing in Eurozone, ECB officials excited to test their new models of inflation expectations:
    • Euro zone inflation surges past ECB target on oil shock
      • ECB's new models may help capture shifts in inflation expectations, blog post says
  • Nasdaq surged nearly 4% as Wall Street closed higher on potential Iran war resolution news
    • Wall Street rallies as traders bet on potential war off-ramp
    • S&P 500 Earnings Estimates Are Surprisingly Rising And $100 WTIC Oil Is Not Expensive

Wednesday, 1 April 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil slides after Trump says US will end its war on Iran soon
      • Auto industry group calls for scrapping US gas tax, adopting vehicle fee
    • Recession odds down sharply after string of better-than-expected economic data
    • US mortgage rates jump to 6.57%, highest since August, MBA says
  • Fed officials thinking oil price spike is short-term event:
    • Exclusive: Fed's Barkin says households, firms still see oil shock through a "short-term lens"
  • Bigger trouble, growth signs developing in China:
    • Factory input costs soar worldwide as Iran war snarls up supply chains
    • China's new home prices rise in March; big cities see seasonal pickup, private survey shows
  • New BOJ official worried about potential for stagflation in Japan's economy:
    • New BOJ board member warns of stagflation risk from Iran war
  • Bigger trouble developing in Eurozone, ECB officials continuing to stand by for action:
    • Euro zone factories see jump in input costs, supply snags, survey shows
    • Euro zone economy may already be on ECB's 'adverse' path, policymaker warns
  • U.S. equities ended higher as hopes of Iran conflict de-escalation lifted sentiment
    • Wall Street ends higher on speculation about end to Iran war

Thursday, 2 April 2026

  • Signs and portents for the U.S. economy:
    • Oil prices jump over 7% after Trump says attacks on Iran will continue
      • Instant View: Investor reactions to Trump's speech on Iran war
    • US labor market posts big gains, war risks mount
      • VIEW US jobs report for March is stronger than expected, likely keeping Fed on sidelines
  • Fed officials disappointed U.S. oil production not ramping up faster:
    • Fed's Logan says US oil producers unlikely to provide near-term relief for consumers
  • Bigger trouble developing in China:
    • China services activity growth cools in March, private PMI shows
  • Former BOJ official thinks supply disruptions may force BOJ to put aside rate hike plans, but they don't want to give them up:
    • BOJ may be overlooking real risk from Iran war, says ex-c.bank official
    • BOJ keeps rate hike door open even as Iran war squeezes firms
  • ECB official starting to worry a bit about Eurozone economy:
    • Energy crisis raises concerns for financial stability, ECB's Panetta warns
      • German institutes cut growth forecast as Iran war drives inflation higher
  • Wall Street ended mixed as investors digested U.S.-Iran war developments
    • Wall Street ends mixed as worries linger before Good Friday break

The Atlanta Fed's GDPNow tool forecast of real GDP growth in 2026-Q1 slowed to +2.0%, declining from the +2.0% growth anticipated a week earlier.

data.title: Ceasefire Hopes Blunt U.S. Ultimatum
data.description:
The US deadline on Tehran for re-opening the Strait of Hormuz has subtly shifted until tomorrow. The holiday-thinned market initially bought dollars and oil, and took risk off in response to the continued attacks and the escalation of US rhetoric. However, negotiations, apparently led by Pakistan, Egypt, and Türkiye for a 45-day ceasefire, have captured the imagination of market participants, even though the negotiators themselves do not appear optimistic.

In quiet turnover, the dollar (DXY) has given up its early gains, and as the North American session is about to begin, the greenback is lower against all the G10 currencies and emerging market currencies. US index futures are trading firmer, and May WTI is off around 1% but is still near $110. It still seems binary. If the hopes are dashed, risk will come off, as the conflict could dramatically escalate.

Prices

G10

• The euro finished the holiday-thinned session before the weekend on a soft note near session lows (~$1.1515). It slipped to $1.1505 before recovering on the back of hope of a ceasefire that will be negotiated. It has taken out the pre-weekend high (~$1.1550) to rise to almost $1.1570. The next important technical area is $1.1600 and then $1.1630-40.

• The conditions for material intervention to support the yen by Japanese officials do not appear present, and the market does not appear to have given up on fishing for the official pain threshold. The dollar edged up to almost JPY159.85, a five-day high, before falling to almost JPY159.30. Last Friday’s low was around JPY159.45. A break of JPY159.00, where options for about $672 mln expire late today, could spur a move to last week’s low near JPY158.30.

• Sterling traded heavily ahead of the weekend and slipped back below $1.32 in North American dealings. Earlier today, it held above the low set near $1.3160 last week, which it had not seen since last November. It recovered though last Friday’s high (~$1.3245) to reach nearly $1.3260. Above there, the next hurdle is around $1.3285, and there are options for GBP475 mln struck at $1.3300 that expire today.

• The US dollar reached a four-month high against the Canadian dollar last week slightly above CAD1.3965. It held below the pre-weekend high, near CAD1.3950, and had returned to last Friday’s low (~CAD1.3915). A break of CAD1.3900 could signal a move toward CAD1.3870.

• The Australian dollar settled about 0.25% higher last week. Still, after a 2.1% sell-off the previous week, it was a faint-hearted bounce. And the Aussie closed below $0.6900. It was initially sold to almost $0.6875 today before it recovered to around $0.6935. Options for A$330 mln at $0.6900 expire today. To confirm the bullish outside up day, the Aussie must close above $0.6915.

EM

• The dollar has found support in the past three sessions near the 20-day moving average, which comes in around MXN17.8350 today. It has been sold a little through MXN17.77 today, its lowest level since March 26. The greenback is posting an outside down day against the peso and must close below MXN17.8250 to confirm it. A convincing break could see MXN17.70 initially.

• The offshore yuan looks like it is going no place quickly. With mainland markets on holiday today, the greenback remains within last Thursday’s range (~CNH6.8725-6.9040). At the same time, its relative stability makes it attractive for some market participants. Some are making the same case about Chinese government bonds. Since the war began, the yield on the 10-year Chinese bond is virtually flat. The 10-year Treasury yield is up 40 bp coming into today, and the 10-year Gilt yield was up almost 60 bp. Japan’s 10-year benchmark yield has risen almost 30 bp, while Germany’s is up by 35 bp.

• The short squeeze engineered by the Reserve Bank of India last week by limiting positions of banks and denying them access to the non-deliverable forward market extended into today’s activity after last Friday’s holiday. The dollar peaked near INR95.1250 on March 30, and today traded down to INR92.78, the lowest level since March 18. It frayed the 20-day moving average (~INR92.83) and settled a little above INR93.06.

Other Markets

• The extended holiday means many equity markets are closed today. Japanese markets themselves were mixed. The Kospi and Indian equities gained more than 1%. The S&P 500 (SPX) and Nasdaq (NDX) futures are trading 0.25-0.50% better.

• The 10-year JGB yield rose 3.5 bp to a new high near 2.43%. It was around 2.11% before the war. The 10-year Treasury yield is firm near 4.35%. It was near 3.94% before the war.

• Gold is trading firmly but is within the pre-weekend range (~$4554-4800). Silver is a little better than flat, slightly above $73.

• Initially, May WTI rose to almost $115.50, a new high, but hope that there would be a ceasefire ahead of the expiration of the US ultimatum pushed the contract a little through $109. It is straddling the $110 area ahead of the North American open.

Data

• US March ISM services survey will be released today. Activity is likely to have slowed, and prices are expected to have risen. Mostly pre-war data is on tap this week. The mid-week release of last month’s FOMC minutes may offer insight into how officials are balancing the risk to both mandates. Still, the futures market is fairly convinced now there will be no change in policy in the coming months.

• Canada sees the March services and composite PMI. Canada’s economy was weak before the war. The composite PMI was above the 50 boom/bust level once last year (October) and was at 47.1 in February. It averaged 47.3 in Q4 '25 and 46.8 in the first two months of 2026.

• Mexican markets re-open after the four-day weekend, and the high-frequency data, including gross fixed investment and private consumption, are from January, making them too old to have much market significance. March domestic vehicle sales are also due. There is a strong seasonal tendency for March sales to improve (18 of the past 20 years). They stood near 118.3k in February.

• India’s final March composite PMI stands at 57.0 (up from 56.5 preliminary estimate). While for most other countries this would be a constructive result, for India it is the weakest since October 2022. The central bank meets at the end of the week. None of the 13 economists who participated in Bloomberg’s survey anticipate a change in the 5.25% repo rate, but the swaps market recognizes the risk of a hike after the central bank has taken measures to curtail the bank’s ability to short or foster short rupee positions.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The YieldMax Trap: Why "Nosebleed" Yields Often Lead To Losses
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Usually, I'm writing about what dividend stocks to buy, with a bias toward high yields. So naturally, people are always asking for my opinion on various dividend investments. One class of popular dividend investments that we haven't touched at High Dividend Opportunities are the rapidly growing class of ETFs that generate huge dividends through options strategies. Perhaps the most popular ones today are the "YieldMax" family of funds.

YieldMax is well known for having nosebleed distributions. With many funds offering yields that are well into the double-digits. 40, 50, even 80%. We've avoided them.

Today, we explain why.

Understand How Your Investments Make Money

Many investors use the phrase "NAV erosion". However, a large number of investors fail to accurately identify what NAV erosion is. Many simply look at lower prices and assume that NAV has been "eroded".

That is too simplistic an assumption because the market is filled with liquid assets that have changing prices. Consider iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), an ETF that holds 20+ year Treasury Bonds. Its NAV has declined from over $130 to $86 over the past 10 years. Does that mean that TLT is "overpaying" or that NAV is "eroding"?

Of course not. The Treasuries that TLT holds are going to pay out their par value at maturity 100% of the time. If they don't, you have bigger issues than what's in your investment portfolio.

TLT's price/NAV changes to reflect what the current long-term interest rates are.

If we go back in time, to when the 20-year Treasury rate was near 5%, TLT was trading about where it is trading today:

TLT's NAV didn't "erode" – the value it trades at changed to reflect current macro-economic conditions. If the 20-year Treasury is ever 1% again, then TLT will be trading over $160.

TLT is a good example because it doesn't carry the complexities of credit risk or realizing losses like funds that invest in equities or debt that has credit risk. TLT holds relatively liquid bonds and doesn't have any material credit risk, making it a very clean tie. If long-term Treasury rates are X, then TLT's price will be Y.

Other funds are more complex, but it highlights the need to understand the moving parts, rather than just looking at NAV and assuming you know the story.

Here are the questions we ask:

  • What assets is the fund invested in?
  • How volatile are the prices of those assets?
  • Are those assets currently trading at relatively high or low valuations?
  • Are the assets liquid?
  • How is the fund deriving cash flow from those assets?
  • Is that cash flow sufficient to cover the distribution?

Covered Call Funds

The YieldMax funds are fundamentally a new iteration of "covered-call" funds. Historically, covered call funds like Global X NASDAQ 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD) generally used a "buy-write" strategy, using 100% ATM call options. In the case of QYLD, using the Nasdaq.

This strategy capped the upside, essentially selling off upside in exchange for a 12% yield. The result is that the price slowly declined.

Yet, if we look at the underlying assets, the Nasdaq has seen incredibly strong performance over this decade. In other words, QYLD is down, but its underlying asset is up – a lot. This is different from the TLT example above, where the fund is down in price, but the underlying assets are down in price as well.

US Treasuries are at multi-decade lows, and the Nasdaq until this month was at all-time highs. Despite the Nasdaq's extraordinary performance (QQQ), QYLD's performance has been relatively poor.

This is NAV erosion. If US Treasuries go back to 2014 levels, TLT will be priced at 2014 levels. If QQQ goes back to 2014 levels, QYLD is going to be much lower than it is today. There is no reasonable scenario where QYLD gets back to $25. That NAV is lost, permanently.

The reason is that covered calls are selling off the upside, and the Nasdaq is volatile. Its price swings a lot. The result is that QYLD has less upside when the Nasdaq is rising; as a result, it always underperforms on upswings.

On downswings, QYLD loses a little less, but it isn't significantly less:

The net result is that QYLD will consistently underperform, and that underperformance is structural and inevitable. QYLD provides a reasonable return over many periods, but investors should keep in mind that they are sacrificing a lot of upside potential, while the downside protection is much more limited than some people assume. QYLD has produced an average CAGR of approximately 9% since inception, while QQQ has produced 20% over the same period. In other words, investors have sacrificed more than half of the upside for cash flow.

The Rise of YieldMax

The YieldMax funds are all relatively new because their strategy wasn't possible until the SEC Rule 18f-4 was finalized in 2020. YieldMax funds use derivatives to create a "synthetic" long position and then sell covered calls on that position.

YieldMax has focused on some of the most volatile stocks in the market, with most of its funds focused on just a single ticker. The idea is a bit like trying to drag race on an icy road.

NAVs have declined rapidly. In just slightly over 3 years, YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy (TSLY) has lost 85% of its price.

And the total return? Like QYLD, it has materially underperformed the benchmark.

TSLY is selling off upside, the same way that QYLD is. And because they use a purely derivative strategy and because they focus on the most volatile individual stocks, it has all the downsides of the strategy amplified. There are many more in the family, all promising high yields from non-dividend growth tech, or low-dividend tech leaders (MSTY) (AMZY) (CONY) (GOOY) (PLTY) (APLY). They have and always will underperform their benchmark.

And this is on a stock that has had an incredible total return during this period. What happens when the benchmark does poorly? Some might think that the nosebleed yield will protect them from downside. It doesn't.

Consider YieldMax's fund that uses C3 AI (AIYY):

Conclusion

Many of the YieldMax funds have had decent returns, and they pay nosebleed dividends. Yet when you look at what the funds do, they have decent returns primarily because the underlying assets have had spectacular returns. If the underlying assets stop having spectacular returns, that's a problem for these funds. They don't protect investors from downside, and they aren't conjuring cash from thin air. They are selling the upside. These Growth stocks are great because they have significant upside.

We believe that many of these Growth stocks are currently overvalued and see increased downside risk. Yet even if we didn't believe that, investors are better off investing directly in the stocks themselves. TSLA is and always will be a materially better investment option than TSLY. NVDA is a materially better investment option than NVDY.

Stop trying to force your Growth stocks into income stocks. Use the right tool for the job. If you want income, buy income investments. Consider something like PIMCO Corporate Income Opportunity Trust (PTY), which provides a very similar but more consistent level of income while providing a similar total return: Source

Yet with one key difference. PTY's NAV is down less than QYLD's, even though the underlying assets that PTY invests in are generally lower priced today than they were 10 years ago. PTY invests in debt, and with interest rates high, debt prices are down. Like TLT, when macro-conditions return to 2015 levels, we can expect PTY's NAV to return to the same general ballpark.

Taking a little time to understand how a fund generates cash flow and the structure that a fund has will go a long way toward making good choices to achieve the goals you have.

If you want income, buy investments that are going to produce income from a recurring source. If you want growth, buy growth stocks that can see their prices go up to infinity and beyond. If you want both, have a mix in your portfolio. For example, here is a portfolio that is 80% PTY and 20% QQQ:

You get better performance, and still a high income.

Don't fall for the trap of buying financially engineered vehicles, trying to convert growth to income or income to growth! You'll usually end up with an investment that is neither.

data.title: Too Cheap To Ignore - 2 Stocks I Think The Market Is Getting Very Wrong
data.description:
Introduction

Ahead of tomorrow’s launch of my Main Street Alpha service on Seeking Alpha (at least, that sentence is valid if you’re reading this article today, on Monday), I thought about a way to bring some more single stocks to your attention. After all, that’s a promise I made in light of all the in-depth Big Picture reports we did in recent months.

However, I didn’t want to make this an article about a few high-yielding stocks I like or something along those lines, or even a stay-away-from-these-stocks-type of article. While I will certainly provide articles like that again in the days/weeks ahead, I wanted to take a little detour today and cover two “riskier” stocks than we usually discuss.

Both are on my watchlist, and both have been punished lately. On top of that, both stand out, as they are nothing compared to the more conservative investments you may hold. In this article, I will cover their business models, explain what type of investor may enjoy them, and explain why I expect both companies to deliver elevated returns going forward.

And, needless to say, I’ll make sure to include Big Picture developments, so even if you plan to never invest in either of these, you’ll still learn something today.

So, let’s get to it!

Mosaic Company (MOS) - A “Special Occasion” Stock

Like everyone else reading this (I think it’s fair to assume that), I like stocks that go up on a very consistent basis. Companies that have figured out how to generate shareholder returns.

Mosaic is not one of these companies.

I was born in June 1995. If my parents had bought MOS stock for me back then, right now it would be worth roughly the same amount as it was worth back then. And, to make matters worse, that includes all dividends paid since then, as the chart below shows a total return price.

That’s not because it has bad management. It’s because Mosaic, which is based in Tampa, Florida, is a giant that produces potash and phosphates. These are fertilizers that farmers all around the globe depend on.

These fertilizers are critical, as we can see above, as they are part of the NPK trio. Without these fertilizers, farmers lose yield, which puts pressure on global food output. However, that doesn’t automatically make MOS a great investment. That’s because fertilizers are commodities.

As I have often written, fertilizers, like most commodities, usually trade barely above the average cost of production. That’s because supply is so abundant. Just think about it this way: if your local town suddenly had 200 used car dealers instead of 10, you can imagine that they all end up selling their cars barely above the price they bought them for. Some companies will have higher margins because they are good at buying cars, while others will go bankrupt.

The same goes for farmers, by the way. As we can see below, crops like corn usually trade barely above the total cost of production. Again, that’s because supply is so abundant.

Generally speaking, this is a low-margin supply chain for most players involved. And, for us consumers, that’s good. Imagine what grocery prices would be like if these companies had software-like margins.

Anyway, this makes most fertilizer companies bad long-term investments.

However, they tend to make for excellent trading vehicles.

Mosaic is an inflation play. That’s how I would describe it in an elevator pitch.

When inflation rises, especially in areas like energy, fertilizer production becomes more expensive, crop prices rise, and efficient players tend to see rising margins. The chart below shows the comparison of MOS’s stock price to the year-over-year inflation rate in the U.S.

I am bullish on inflation, especially because I expect oil to remain in a (volatile) long-term uptrend. Sure, once the Iran War ends, oil will likely come down a bit. But adjusted for these “events,” I think we’re looking at inflation acceleration and growth broadening. Both of these events are bullish for Mosaic.

For example, in December, MOS traded at $24. Before the Iran War started, it traded at $30. That’s a 25% gain completely unrelated to the war that nobody saw coming. That’s the thesis I am looking for, as the market started to price in higher cyclical growth and rotated into cyclical value. The war in Iran is just noise for me.

On top of that, phosphates have seen more favorable supply developments, as the data below (supported by Argus, IFA, CRU, Mosaic, and third-party research) shows an increasing supply/demand gap in recent years. I believe this is a reason why prices have become more sensitive to headlines. That’s good for MOS and its peers.

During its 4Q25 call, the company confirmed this (pre-Iran War):

Looking ahead, phosphate supply and demand dynamics are supportive as China continues to restrict exports to prioritize domestic demand and lithium iron phosphate battery demand continues to consume an even larger share of the world's phosphoric acid. Potash markets remain balanced with prices that appeal to the world's farmers and fertilizer producers alike. As we look at 2026, we expect global potash shipments to approach record levels driven by broad-based demand across most key geographies. And as a result, we expect to continue producing at high operating rates. While credit constraints remain a challenge in Brazil, expanding planted acreage and rising crop yields bode well for long-term Brazilian fertilizer demand. Demand also remains strong in other key growing regions of the world, including China and India. - MOS 4Q25 Earnings Call

Moreover, with regard to its dividend, MOS has a yield of 3.4%. This year, analysts expect close to $700 million in free cash flow, which would translate to an FCF yield of 8.4%. That’s a cash payout ratio of just 40%.

In general, MOS is cheap, even in light of the fact that it’s driven by fertilizer prices. It trades at just 12x earnings. Six months ago, analysts saw a path to $3.30 in 2028 EPS. Now, that number is just $2.13, mostly due to macro headwinds that I expect to turn around.

If I’m even remotely right about higher inflation and growth, I think we’ll see EPS way above $3.00, which would give us a fair stock price target of at least $36 to $40 in a base case.

How fast these developments can unfold was visible in 2025, when the stock went to $38.

I expect to see that again this year.

Kinsale Capital Group (KNSL) - This One’s A Compounder

Mosaic may be a trading vehicle. This one is a compounder.

It’s a company I have briefly mentioned in prior articles, yet almost never given the attention it deserves. This stock may yield just 0.3%, yet it has a payout ratio of just 3% and a major focus on growth. For its total return, that is much better than a 2-3% dividend yield.

Kinsale is an insurance company. However, it’s a highly specialized player, as it operates in the E&S Industry, which means excess and surplus. These are operations that focus on “hard to place” and “non-standard” risks that other insurance companies tend to avoid.

Think of insurance for amusement parks, roofing operations, transportation of certain goods, and other things that are highly specialized. These need insurance, too, as you can imagine.

In this market, it has a market share of 1.4%, according to its January 2026 presentation that used 2023 S&P Global data (see below).

What makes Kinsale so special, besides its unusual operating area, is how efficient it is. Through things like proprietary technology, they have figured out how to grow extremely effectively, which has resulted in an expense ratio of less than 21% in 2025. Most of its peers run ratios in the mid-30s or even above 40%.

In general, their profitability is way above P&C and E&S peers, as we can see below.

The company is also aggressively deploying AI, as it said during its earnings call. This includes bots and agents that do business processes and scan deals. I have to say that before I started researching for this article, KNSL wasn’t in my AI basket. Now, it is, as I think it’s a great AI adopter for the years ahead, that is one of the best examples of how AI tools can help with data generation and analysis.

With that said, being more efficient is an advantage in more than one way. For example, because they control expenses so well, they can remain extremely disciplined in their underwriting process, as they do not have to sacrifice quality to support growth.

If you’re a small insurance company with low margins, you (more or less) are forced to grow by taking on as many deals as possible. Especially in this business, which can end in tears.

Essentially, KNSL has found a favorable balance between risk, growth, and returns.

This is also critical for the market cycle. Right now, the industry is profitable, which attracts new companies. Because of more insurance supply, prices drop. This makes the industry less profitable, which causes companies to exit. Then, it becomes more profitable again.

This is like a commodity cycle, except we are dealing with insurance supply and demand.

Right now, we see to be in the phase where new competition is emerging, as KNSL explained during its earnings call:

Yes. I would say, in general, we're in a competitive moment in the insurance cycle. With the level of competition varying quite a bit from one, if you will, market segment to the next. The commercial property, we've been talking about the larger Southeastern wind accounts. That's an example of one of the most competitive areas. And then Stuart, a few minutes ago listed a number of areas where we're still seeing very reasonably strong premium growth and upward movement in pricing. So we feel very positive about the business overall, given our underwriting and our cost advantages. - KNSL 4Q25 Earnings Call

This is unfavorable for its stock price, as whenever new competition emerges, the market tends to adjust the multiple it’s willing to pay for a company. The best example of that is software in light of AI disruption risks.

Right now, KNSL trades at a blended P/E ratio of slightly less than 18x. That’s a mile below its long-term multiple of 33x. That number was based on explosive growth, as it saw more than 80% growth in 2021, 36% growth in 2022, 60% growth in 2023, and so forth.

This year, analysts expect 3% growth, potentially followed by 10% and 6% growth in 2027 and 2028, respectively. That’s the impact of new competition. While I cannot estimate how long that will last, I do like the valuation and believe that growth will end up higher than expected if I am right about the cyclical growth recovery. Such a scenario would boost insurance demand from manufacturers, transportation companies, energy companies, chemical plants, and so many other players.

Sure, insurance supply is also higher, but it will likely end up providing a positive surprise to earnings in the year/years ahead.

And, to be honest, I prefer E&S insurance over healthcare, especially because of regulations and pressure on affordability in the healthcare system. E&S may be riskier, but when investing in KNSL, I think the odds are in investors’ favor.

Takeaway

I don’t cover a lot of risky stocks. However, today, I think it is the perfect opportunity to cover two attractive opportunities. Mosaic is an inflation play in agriculture. Kinsale is a specialized insurance stock.

While Mosaic has general pricing risks due to its reliance on commodity prices, and Kinsale is stuck in a cycle of new industry supply that pressures pricing, I think both companies are likely in a bottoming process and winners of my “cyclical value” thesis.

Don’t buy them if you’re a very conservative investor, but if you look for potential alpha and diversification, one of these may be right for you.

Personally, I have both on my watchlist and may make MOS a holding of the Main Street Alpha community portfolio, but more on that soon.

data.title: Soleno Acquisition: Assessing The $50 Per Share Price Tag
data.description:
Thesis

As you know, Neurocrine Biosciences (NBIX) is said to be in advanced negotiations to acquire Soleno Therapeutics (SLNO). The deal is tipped to value the company at just over $2.5 billion, or in the low-to-mid $50s per share. We were also told that discussions seem to be progressing pretty quickly, with a potential agreement possibly coming as soon as Monday. Now, this would actually be Neurocrine's first major deal and would, of course, add Soleno's key asset to their list, which is the first approved treatment for extreme hunger associated with Prader-Willi syndrome. In terms of that particular asset's potential, some analysts estimate that it could generate up to $2.3 billion in peak annual sales. As for the stock price, Soleno shares have surged almost 30% over the past week on takeover speculation.

The buy case here for me would ultimately come down to owning a de-risked, first-in-class rare disease asset. It's tipped to have significant pricing power and a pretty clear path to rapid revenue scale, at what still looks like a rather modest multiple of peak sales. As you know, Vykat XR already has regulatory approval and some very strong clinical durability, which is set to give it a somewhat monopoly-like positioning in PWS. Where I see the real upside would be in pipeline-in-a-product expansion into some other metabolic and obesity indications. This is, of course, much longer-term, but even modest success in other uses could multiply the TAM.

Pipeline setup

As far as their pipeline goes, Soleno is pretty well concentrated on a single core asset, diazoxide choline extended-release (DCCR). DCCR is already commercially approved as Vykat XR in the U.S. as of 2025 and would be the company's lead and essentially their only late-stage/marketed product. It's designed to treat hyperphagia, extreme, life-threatening hunger, in patients with Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS). Now, this syndrome is a rare genetic disorder with no prior approved therapies specifically targeting appetite dysregulation, which is why the candidate's approval generated so much interest. Previously, we saw the drug completed a full clinical program spanning Phase 1 and Phase 2 studies and a Phase 3 program demonstrating improvements not only in hyperphagia but also in related metabolic and behavioural symptoms, which is what supported its NDA and eventual FDA approval.

Vykat XR

Just to go over some background for Vykat XR briefly, it's actually a reformulated version of the older drug diazoxide. Mechanistically speaking, it works by activating ATP-sensitive potassium channels, particularly those in pancreatic beta cells and in the hypothalamus. In activating these channels, it leads to suppression of insulin secretion and modulation of neuropeptide signalling tied to hunger/satiety. Now, this is extremely useful since in Prader-Willi syndrome, we usually see hyperphagia driven by dysregulated hypothalamic signalling and lots of abnormal metabolic hormones such as leptin and insulin. So DCCR would then act upstream by reducing those insulin levels and improving leptin/adiponectin balance, thus dampening that persistent hunger signal.

What also makes this drug very attractive from a commercial standpoint is that it is administered orally and only once daily in an extended-release tablet. And given the chronic nature of PWS and the need for steady metabolic control, that long-release aspect is pretty critical. As for the clinical side, DCCR has demonstrated pretty broad, durable efficacy across Phase 2/3 programs and long-term extensions. Patients have shown statistically significant reductions in hyperphagia scores, often in the 45% to 55% range in terms of improvement. We also saw improvements in behaviour such as anxiety, aggression, and compulsivity, which was nice to see. But what was most notable to me was that these effects persisted over multiple years, and still managed to show continued improvements in both appetite control and overall disease severity. So that tells me the drug is not merely symptomatic here but may actually be modifying underlying metabolic dysfunction.

As for early revenue, some may see the launch as a bit of a concern in terms of launch quality/durability. What we know is that Vykat XR grew from about $66 million in 3Q25 to about $91.7 million in 4Q25. However, new patient start forms fell to just 207 in 4Q25, and no updated active patient numbers were given to us. So this would suggest some potential slowing after the initial pent-up demand. It's also worth noting that there are some side effects, such as oedema and hyperglycemia, that led to about a 12% discontinuation rate and likely dose reductions. So this could potentially compress revenue per patient even if the active counts hold. Below is from CEO Anish Bhatnagar.

Importantly, the real-world safety profile of VYKAT XR continues to mirror our expectations and the clinical long-term safety profile of the drug. This is significant when you reflect on the complexity of PWS and also serious comorbidities of the very vulnerable and fragile patient population. The cumulative launch to date discontinuation rate of VYKAT XR related to adverse events was approximately 12% as of the end of the fourth quarter and the total discontinuation rate was about 15%. As stated earlier, we expect a long-term discontinuation rate of 15% to 20%"

Other indications

So if we take that combination of neurobehavioral and metabolic effects, the buy case here for Neurocrine is that DCCR could possibly expand beyond just PWS into other rare genetic obesities or broader hyperphagic/metabolic disorders. This could especially be true for those involving leptin-insulin dysregulation. In fact, diazoxide-based approaches have already been explored in some other conditions, like monogenic obesity syndromes, which could support biological plausibility for label expansion later on. But what I see as making Vykat XR meaningfully different here from those earlier diazoxide-based approaches is not the core mechanism, but rather its ability to consistently engage central/brain pathways at very tolerable exposures. Historically, this has been the limiting factor.

You see, earlier uses of diazoxide, which included obesity/hyperinsulinemia studies, only relied on immediate-release formulations. These were typically oral suspensions and were dosed multiple times throughout the day. So the result here was very peaky, unstable drug levels, which led to quite a few tolerability issues like oedema and hyperglycemia, to name a couple. Those tolerability issues usually appeared before they could sustain the kind of continuous hypothalamic KATP channel activation we need to meaningfully affect someone's appetite regulation. On the flip side, DCCR is actually a crystalline salt, with an extended-release formulation specifically engineered for slow absorption down the GI tract. So that's what allows it to produce such a stable concentration and gives it improved bioavailability. And that stability is not just a convenience feature here; it's what allows for chronic modulation of neuropeptide Y (NPY)/AgRP neurons in the hypothalamus, which we often see as the central drivers of hunger signalling. In my opinion, those earlier diazoxide approaches likely failed to show consistent efficacy in broader obesity, simply because they couldn't maintain this steady central engagement without having unacceptable side effects.

Commercial potential

So from a commercial standpoint, DCCR was FDA-approved back in March 2025, which means revenue generation is still in the very early stages of launch. However, the drug has an extremely strong positioning for a rare disease drug, since it's the first and only approved therapy specifically targeting hyperphagia in PWS. Some analysts' estimates of $2.3 to $2.5 billion peak annual sales would then suggest a TAM in that range for the current labelled indication. And as far as orphan drug pricing goes, this seems quite consistent. As per Soleno's estimates, they put the TAM at around 10,000 people with PWS at 4 years and older. The disease itself actually has a higher prevalence at around 12,000 in the U.S., but not all would be eligible for treatment with Vykat XR.

In reality, the market is actually even more concentrated than those numbers would suggest. As per Soleno's reporting, only about 300 healthcare providers manage the majority of PWS patients. So they would have the biggest influence on treatment decisions for several thousand more. Now, this is pretty critical because it means the TAM is not just defined by patient numbers here, but also by a highly concentrated prescriber base. That base is already well defined, so it makes the market a lot more accessible commercially despite being small. So when analysts translate this into revenue potential, it would support the higher TAM/peak sales narrative in that it's a relatively small, well-identified patient pool and treated by a limited number of specialists. But it also means the drug will have to come with an extremely high per-patient annual pricing and long treatment duration.

Now, you're probably wondering why Neurocrine would pay $2.5 billion if the TAM is tipped to be the same amount. Well, the real upside here is in expansion. If DCCR successfully moves into some adjacent indications, such as other genetic obesity disorders, hypothalamic obesity, or even broader metabolic disease subsets, the TAM could then expand several-fold. And that doesn't have to come with a first-to-market, dominating move. Even if we saw just modest penetration into larger obesity-related subpopulations, it could push the effective TAM much higher. But this would, of course, require new clinical trials, regulatory approvals, and differentiation from entrenched obesity therapies like the current line-up of GLP-1 agonists.

Valuation

As far as a fair valuation goes for Soleno, the current profile would reflect a classic early commercial-stage biotech transition. The backwards-looking metrics seem quite expensive, whilst the forward-looking ones compress pretty rapidly as revenue scales. On a trailing basis, the stock would screen as very expensive, with an EV to sales of about 8.3x and price to sales of 10.5x, which are well above the healthcare sector median of about 3.4x. I think the more relevant lens here is the forward estimates; however, with EV to EBITDA at 6.5x and EV to EBIT sitting at 7.6x, which actually screens discounted against the sector averages. Even the forward price-to-earnings of 10x for 2026 compresses to about 3x by 2029, which would suggest the market is pricing in a very rapid earnings ramp from Vykat XR adoption. We can back this up with a hefty consensus EPS growth of around 900% into 2026. Now, this is unusually high but also pretty typical for a company moving from near-zero revenue to meaningful commercialisation.

If we look at this set-up from a strategic/acquisition standpoint, that $2.0 billion market cap and around $1.6 billion enterprise value are arguably quite below what a fully de-risked rare-disease asset with $2 to $2.5 billion peak sales potential might actually command. This would be especially true given the high-margin, concentrated prescriber base and basically monopoly positioning in PWS. That being said, a takeout price in the low-to-mid $50s per share ($2.5 billion plus equity value) would give us a 25% to 35% premium to the current trading range. This would still equate to roughly 1.0 to 1.2x peak sales, which is not that aggressive by rare-disease M&A standards, in which we usually see 2x to 4x peak sales not being uncommon for high-confidence assets. So for me, the key debate is risk here, in that the market is still discounting things like launch execution, payer uptake, and single-asset concentration, which explains why current multiples aren't actually richer despite those strong forward projections. That being said, if a buyer like Neurocrine underwrites the launch curve and lifecycle expansion potential, a $50/share acquisition price could then look not only realistic but arguably conservative, particularly if early prescription trends support a bull case like the rapid revenue ramp up implied in those forward estimates.

Looking ahead

So overall, Soleno is at a bit of an interesting inflexion point here. It has a de-risked, first-in-class asset with strong, very strong pricing power and early profitability. However, the launch is showing some signs of normalisation after initial pent-up demand. That being said, the market seems to be appropriately balancing both sides along with acquisition talk news. They're discounting execution risk while still recognising the multi-billion-dollar potential of Vykat XR and the upside if expansion opportunities materialise. So against that backdrop, a $50 per share takeout does look somewhat justified on current fundamentals and could ultimately prove conservative if the drug delivers on the longer-term growth trajectory I laid out.

data.title: Gladstone Capital: Buy One Of The Best BDCs During Trouble
data.description:
Over the past few months, business development companies have taken the spotlight as one of the most dramatic asset classes. What started as pocketed bankruptcy trouble among two large borrowers has quickly spread into fears of wider systemic trouble.

Similar to what hit non-traded REITs in the rising interest rate era, a massive wave of redemptions has hit non-traded business development companies, initially those managed by Blue Owl (OWL). Other firms, including Cliffwater (CCLFX), who became famous for introducing private credit to the masses, have seen substantial troubles in their lineup of funds, including a liquidity gate in their flagship interval fund.

More likely than not, we are entering a period where we will find out who has been swimming naked. As the dominoes begin to fall, we are quickly seeing some of the weaker publicly traded business development companies begin to take damage. As a result, the sector is falling apart quickly.

Today, we circle back to Gladstone Capital Company (GLAD) and talk about why this business development company is well positioned to weather the troubled storm.

Review of Prior Coverage

It is hard to believe that it has been nearly five years since I last covered GLAD. In the post pandemic era, I covered business development companies as they recovered from the extraordinary pandemic volatility. What many thought would be a challenging time for business development companies quickly emerged into the golden era as interest rates increased and default rates remained low.

My prior coverage went over GLAD's business in detail. For those who are new to GLAD, it may be a helpful place to start.

The pattern of GLAD's performance over the past five years is indicative of the broader business development company market. As the secured overnight financing rate increased, defaults fell, and demand for private loans increased, GLAD found itself in the flow state. The following four years were an extraordinary time for GLAD and other business development companies.

As often happens, the picture has reversed much more quickly than it came together. Over a period of just one year, GLAD shares have seen one of their largest declines in the company's long history. Looking across the sector, the damage has left no stone unturned. Every business development company has been damaged, except for a lucky few.

Interestingly enough, GLAD's sister fund, Gladstone Investment Company (GAIN) has outperformed the business development company sector significantly over the past year.

As sector-wide trouble begins to reach individual businesses, the question becomes whether these troubles will affect each company uniformly. I would venture to guess the answer is no, as was the case with REITs just a few short years ago. Some REITs have struggled significantly while others have thrived, even within the same subsector. For example, compare Global Net Lease (GNL) to Agree Realty (ADC) over the past five years.

The answer mostly comes down to internal factors, including balance sheet management. In this case, GNL had much more debt and inferior assets compared to ADC.

I often see a misconception that publicly traded REITs and business development companies are a pure reflection of the assets that they own. This does not consider the substantial work and prudence required to maintain a healthy balance sheet over time.

As we head deeper into trouble, I believe that GLAD is in a better position than many competitors to weather the storm. Let's explore why in greater detail.

What Makes GLAD Different?

One of the most important considerations is what makes a business development company different. What makes a company unique can be big or small. Some carry more debt; others focus more heavily on equity investments. Some are internally managed, and some are externally managed. At the end of the day, these differences are nuanced, but they matter.

GLAD has some key differentiating factors that will likely help the company outperform the broader sector over the next several years.

As trouble begins to reach many of these sponsor-backed companies that heavily depend on private funding to continue their growth recapitalization strategies, the financial framework is beginning to show signs of stress. Across the sector, nonaccrual rates have begun to rise. As certain borrowers begin to show signs of stress, including a rising appearance of what's known as payment-in-kind interest. Software has been one of the most troubled pockets of borrowers.

One of the most important differentiating points for GLAD, is that the company has very little exposure to the software sector. Instead, the company has focused more heavily on originating with companies that run more traditional businesses.

Healthcare, manufacturing, and diversified conglomerates are some of the largest borrowers from GLAD. This comes in large contrast to many of the venture technology borrowers, which have stuffed the portfolios of larger business development companies, such as FS KKR (FSK).

The Q1 earnings call had an analyst ask specifically regarding GLAD's exposure to AI and data center-related borrowers. I loved the answer…

Just curious if you're seeing in your origination funnel more opportunities for AI and data center-related opportunities, just how you kind of view this trend, if it's likely a longer-term trend or if you're watching it more cautiously. Just curious on your take there.

Robert Marcotte President

We don't directly invest in data centers. That's a big boys game. What was Google's announcement today, $180 billion or whatever the number was. We used to do that, but that's not really something that we see in the lower middle market. We do see some of the spend from those projects coming through in our portfolio. It might be bus bars that are going into data centers that, frankly, IMX does make. And certainly, there are construction or HVAC or air handling services that might come through to some of those segments. We are very cautious about the sustainability. There's an awful lot of folks jumping into that market. And we are watching the reliance on that end of the market as we think about the play, but we are not directly investing in what I would say is a significant reliance on the continuation of that investment spend. That's just not where our companies particularly play.

Should the ongoing troubles end up being the canary in the coal mine for business development companies, having a portfolio that's focused away from one of the fastest-growing, but most troubled borrower segments is something that will look good in hindsight. Going further, around 80% of the company's portfolio is sponsor-backed. The remainder are affiliated with GLAD or GAIN, or privately owned. This gives GLAD a greater degree of control over that 20%.

Another, even more critical consideration is the company's debt load. As is usually the case, some of the higher-leverage business development companies have been the first to reach trouble. With non-accrual rates beginning to increase, higher-leverage business development companies are quickly coming under pressure as the growth strategy deteriorates.

GLAD has one of the lightest leverage loads in the industry, with a debt-to-equity ratio of just 85% as of year-end. Recently, this ratio has increased to around 1x, given the recent share declines.

Compare this to the industry average of more than 120%, and the capitalization of GLAD looks much more conservative. With a portfolio fair value of just over $900 million, this means that the company's total debt load is around $400 million.

GLAD has plenty of room to wait out ongoing volatility, and their low leverage provides more cushion. This has already begun to help the company, as the most recent quarterly earnings began to show some stress. The amount of stress inside of earnings was sufficient to cause a substantial sell-off for GLAD shares this year.

I see opportunity afoot as shares continue to move below net asset value and briefly pass below $17 per share.

Valuation Quicksand

Over the past several years, I have identified valuation as a considerable risk factor for BDCs. During the golden era, the growth and earning power of business development companies fueled the market to assign a premium price.

Companies like Main Street Capital (MAIN) quickly became the biggest offenders in the overvaluation game. Even companies like GLAD were caught up in the mix. If we were to rewind the clock and look at the past 12 months, we would note that GLAD had an abnormally high valuation. The company's elevated stock price had advanced far beyond net asset value.

At the peak, GLAD reached a price-to-book value of over 1.3x.

The bigger they are, the harder they fall, and GLAD was no different. Over the past year, the decline of GLAD shares has outpaced the decline in net asset value by a considerable margin. This means the price of GLAD shares has been underperforming the actual underlying portfolio by a significant margin.

Today, shares of GLAD are priced at a price-to-net asset value of around 0.85x. This is a far cry from the premium to net asset value assigned to shares 12 months ago. Part of this wild shift in pricing is the market's prediction that as GLAD's portfolio continues to be marked to market, and more non-accruals emerge, net asset value is likely to fall further.

If this draconian scenario does not take place, and we are to believe that this is a liquidity issue and not a borrower issue, the upside for a company like GLAD could be considerable. The company's dividend yield is still above 10%, even considering the dividend cut at the end of last year, which the company noted stemmed largely from rate cuts.

Risk Factors

Another important question to consider is what could go wrong. In the case of business development companies, it looks like a lot. What was once a niche asset class has quickly become one of the most attention grabbing investment categories. As the industry grew, so did the risk factors.

It turns out, a risk study that was published by the Federal Reserve several years ago, appears to be taking shape. The report noted that as more borrowers turned towards business development, companies, direct lenders, and private credit funds, risk factors were beginning to elevate. The growth of the industry has been exponential in the post pandemic era.

Debt coverage ratios were declining, loan spreads were decreasing inappropriately, and even then non-accrual rates appeared to be rising early.

Fast forward a couple of years, and these risk factors begin to age like a fine wine.

Conclusion

As turmoil continues to reach the business development company sector, I believe GLAD has a better portfolio and a better balance sheet than many of their competitors. When looking at how to navigate hard times, looking towards strong fundamentals is often the solution. As we learned with REITs, not all of these companies will be treated equally by the market.

More likely than not, all will fall when trouble hits, but those who re-emerge from the ashes will be determined by who was better positioned at the starting line. From my perspective, GLAD has a battle worthy balance sheet and a portfolio of traditionalist businesses that will likely be more immune to the ongoing software volatility. GLAD earns a Buy rating.

data.title: Intel: 3 Reasons Why The $14.2B Apollo Deal Is Significant
data.description:
Intel (INTC) made a major announcement last week that could cause some new excitement for the firm's shares in the near future: the chipmaker, which strategically reduced its manufacturing footprint in 2024 in order to conserve cash and focus on its restructuring, just announced that it repurchased a 49% joint venture stake related to an Ireland-based manufacturing plant from Apollo Global Management that will bring its ownership stake back to 100%.

The news is significant in its impact not only on investor perceptions, but it could add significantly to Intel’s already improving turnaround prospects. The chip enterprise is pivoting to a fab-centric business model due to surging demand for AI-optimized processors. I like the Apollo announcement, but for the time being I will maintain a hold rating until the firm is seeing narrowing operating losses in its chip manufacturing unit.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Intel a strong buy for more than two years (up to January 2026) as I believed the chipmaker had a good chance to turn its business around, especially as it was successful in securing the financial help of strategic anchor investors. Intel has fallen behind AMD (AMD) in the server CPU market as well as Nvidia (NVDA) in the data center market, however, which presented unique challenges that required asset sales -- such as the Fab 34 in Ireland -- and significant layoffs.

I downgraded Intel to hold in January due to persistent losses in the foundry unit. Intel's Apollo deal signals for the first time in years that Intel's business prospects are improving, especially as demand for AI chips keeps surging. With Intel cashed up after a number of strategic investments by other investors last year, I am cautiously optimistic that the chipmaker will be able to improve its margin profile.

Intel’s $14.2B Apollo deal is significant for 3 reasons

In a major announcement on April 1, 2026, Intel said that it was buying back a 49% stake in a joint venture related to a manufacturing facility located in Ireland. Only three years ago, the chipmaker sold Fab 34 to Apollo Global Management for $11.2B in a bid to raise cash for its uncertain restructuring at the time. Since Intel is now expanding its chip manufacturing business and its AI-focused CPU business is gaining momentum, Intel has decided to repurchase this 49% joint venture stake for $14.2B, allowing the chip enterprise to regain full operational and economic control.

The asset sale will give the private equity company a solid $3.0B investment gain for a small 3-year holding period, but it will also have some very significant benefits for Intel, which has been playing defense essentially since 2023. The transaction, as per the press statement, will be mainly funded by Intel’s cash on its balance sheet (approximately $7.7B) as well as $6.5B in additional debt. Intel has plenty of cash after both the U.S. government and Nvidia (NVDA) made strategic investments into the chip enterprise last year, worth a total of $15.0B ($10.0B from the U.S. government and $5.0B from Nvidia).

Currently, Intel is sitting on a cash pile of $37.4B, showing a much improved balance sheet and liquidity position.

The deal with Apollo, in my opinion, has three distinct advantages that aid the firm's recovery prospects:

  1. The Irish manufacturing plant is set to benefit from growing utilization going forward, as the AI boom is driving massive demand for the Core Ultra and Xeon 6 processors manufactured there. Intel is going all in on Core Ultra and Xeon 6 production in 2026 as part of its AI-driven growth strategy in the CPU segment. A full ownership position is set to give the company more operational control and could lead to an acceleration of growth, potentially, in Intel's core chip manufacturing business.
  2. The deal marks an important turning point for Intel from a sentiment and outlook perspective. While the company sold its 49% stake to Apollo in 2024, the repurchase of the joint venture stake signals that the firm’s balance sheet strength has significantly improved, which is making management bolder to more aggressively endorse its AI-centric growth. The reason for this, as mentioned above, is that Nvidia and the U.S. government made an equity investment into the chipmaker, adding crucial liquidity to Intel's accounts.
  3. Intel’s re-acquisition of its manufacturing plant gives the company vital autonomy to boost not only its CPU production, but it is also set to accelerate its 18A process node roadmap, which is critical for the chipmaker in order to compete more effectively with key competitor Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM). Intel's Panther Lake CPU is built on the 18A process, and the company seeks to double down on this manufacturing strategy going forward, creating tailwinds for its growth.

Intel's growth outlook is improving, and I will be looking forward to a gross profit margin stabilization following the deal and, hopefully, a successive margin improvement in the chipmaker's core business after the deal closes. Intel has guided for the transaction with Apollo to be accretive to earnings per-share, which is due to the fact that the company will no longer have to share profits with its minority joint venture partner.

Intel’s valuation

Compared to AMD and Nvidia, Intel is trading at a much higher forward earnings multiplier because the company is facing temporarily suppressed EPS related to its ongoing restructuring. Shares of Intel are currently trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 53.2X which places the chipmaker significantly above its rivals in the CPU/GPU business. Nvidia is trading at just 16.0X forward earnings, while AMD is priced at a forward 20.1X P/E ratio.

The main reason for this valuation discrepancy is that Intel recognized major restructuring and asset write-down charges in Q3’23 which kicked off a multi-year restructuring for the chipmaker and which depressed analyst expectations about Intel's earnings growth ever since.

In my last work on Intel, I calculated a fair value for the chipmaker's shares of $51, which resulted in a hold rating at the time. Currently, Intel's share price sits at $50.38, so the chipmaker is currently fully priced. However, I do see upside in the fair value estimate under the condition that the full ownership position in Fab 34 will help Intel accelerate growth in its chip manufacturing business and support gross margin expansion.

Risks with Intel

From a transaction point of view, I believe the acquisition of the 49% minority equity ownership stake in the Irish manufacturing plant is a very low-risk endeavor for Intel. The chip enterprise already owns the remaining 51%, and since its cash situation has materially improved since last year, execution risks are very low. While Intel is able to expand its manufacturing business, meaning it is set to grow its revenues in the manufacturing segment, Intel still needs to improve its gross margin profile in order to be able to convince investors that its fab-centric turnaround story is gaining steam.

Final thoughts

Intel’s $14.2B deal with private equity giant Apollo Global Management is a step in the right direction, in my opinion, because it will allow Intel to scale the most in-demand AI products, such as the Ultra Core and Xeon processors for its AI computers. I believe the deal is also a positive for Intel, as it gains more autonomy over its chip manufacturing, which could clear the path for an accelerating pace of production as well as higher gross profits. Further, with Intel being in a much more stable situation financially, following two major anchor investments from the U.S. government and Nvidia last year, the re-acquisition of a manufacturing plant could signal improving confidence in Intel's refined business strategy.

data.title: I Can't Imagine Retiring On Dividends Without These Compounding Machines
data.description:
When looking to retire passively on dividends, one of the best strategies is to set up a well-diversified, low-fee portfolio that is filled with high-quality companies that combine attractive current yields with inflation-beating dividend growth outlooks. Then you can simply buy and hold for the long term while letting the dividends flow in.

One of the best funds for building the foundations of such a portfolio is the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). However, by itself, it has several deficiencies that can be addressed by buying other high-quality dividend compounders. This article details why SCHD is such a good core component for a retirement portfolio that aims to be relatively hands-off and generate sufficient dividends over the long term to cover living expenses, and what some of the best diversification tools are for building such a portfolio.

Why SCHD Stands Apart

SCHD is one of the best dividend growth machines out there because it combines a very low 0.06% expense ratio, which is among the very cheapest in the entire ETF universe and is lower than many dividend funds out there. We're also being well diversified across blue chip dividend growth stocks, as it holds about 100 blue chip dividend payers that are selected based on their yield, payout ratio, dividend growth, and cash flow.

Another perk is that it has a portfolio that is well diversified across other sectors, including:

  • Health care (XLV)

  • Financials (XLF)

  • Energy (XLE)

  • Consumer staples (XLP)

  • Industrials (XLI)

Not only that, but its dividend yield is around 3.5%, which is much higher than many of its peers like Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG), Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM), iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO), and WisdomTree US Quality Dividend Growth Fund (DGRW).

SCHD's Unmatched Track Record

Perhaps best of all is the fact that SCHD has a 10-year annualized dividend growth rate of well over 10%. Additionally, the vast majority of its dividends are qualified dividends, which makes them relatively tax-efficient. Thus, if you're looking for a combination of diversification, quality of underlying holdings, track record of generating very strong dividend growth, and decent current yield while paying a low expense ratio, SCHD is literally as good as it gets. The tax efficiency makes it a suitable holding for both a tax-advantaged and a taxable investment account. SCHD is thus a great foundation for a portfolio of an investor who is looking to retire on dividends, especially one that requires minimal maintenance.

Filling the Gaps With Higher-Yield Sectors

In particular, I think diversifying into sectors like MLPs (AMLP), REITs (VNQ), BDCs (BIZD), and infrastructure (IGF) can make a lot of sense because these are all sectors that SCHD lacks much exposure to, and they tend to offer higher dividend yields, which work together with SCHD to create a better diversified and higher yielding portfolio while still generating very attractive dividend growth that should outpace inflation over time.

The MLP King

In the midstream space, perhaps there's no better option for buying and holding for the long term with minimal monitoring necessary than Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). It operates 50,000 miles of pipeline, 27 fractionators, 21 deepwater docks, 45 gas processing trains, and 300 MM bbls of liquid storage. In particular, it has a dominant position in the NGL value chain, as 55% of its gross operating margin comes from NGLs. This network is fully integrated from the wellhead to the export terminal. Additionally, it is the largest individual LPG exporter in the world and supplies 15% of global waterborne LPG exports. Not only that, but its natural gas processing contracts are generally long-term fee-based, as 89% of them can include a fee component, while 90% of long-term contracts include inflation escalation provisions.

Its balance sheet is also rock solid, as it has the highest credit rating in the entire midstream space of A-, while its leverage ratio of 3.3 times is quite conservative for its large size and is likely to fall even further as it has numerous growth projects to the tune of $4.8 billion under construction that should come online soon. It also has $5.2 billion worth of liquidity, a 17-year weighted average term to maturity on its debt, and it has strong growth ahead of it, thanks to its major growth projects coming online in the coming quarters and years, while also benefiting from energy export and AI-driven natural gas and natural gas liquid demands. EPD expects to generate significant free cash flow in the coming years that it plans to allocate towards increased unit repurchases and debt reduction, both of which in turn should increase its distributable cash flow per unit, which in turn should drive accelerating distribution growth. It already has a stellar distribution growth track record to the tune of 27 straight years, which further validates its position as a great buy-and-hold income investment for the long term. With a distribution coverage ratio of about 1.7 times, a current yield of nearly 6%, its stellar balance sheet, its well-diversified and defensively positioned business model, and strong growth outlook, EPD is an excellent buy-and-hold income compounder, especially for someone looking to retire on passive income from dividends without having to manage the portfolio very actively.

The Ultimate Sleep-Well-At-Night REIT

For REIT exposure, Realty Income (O) is about as good as it gets for a long-term sleep-well-at-night income compounder. There are several reasons for this:

First of all, it has a huge real estate empire comprising over 15,500 properties spread across 10 countries, 92 industries, 1,761 tenants, and a very high occupancy ratio of 98.9%. It also implements a triple net lease business model that delivers very predictable recurring cash flows and makes it a bond proxy, especially given how broadly diversified its portfolio is. It also has a stellar credit rating of A- from S&P and an equivalent A3 rating from Moody's.

When you combine that with the fact that 91% of its portfolio operates non-discretionary, service-oriented, and/or low-price-point businesses, and its stellar track record through COVID and the Great Financial Crisis, it is about as sleep well at night as it gets in the REIT space.

Thus, it is not surprising that O has grown its dividend for 31 straight years, making it a dividend aristocrat, and it pays out its dividends monthly, which is especially attractive for many retirees. Its dividend has grown at a 4.2% compounded annual growth rate since 1994, which means it has consistently grown its dividend at a rate that meets or beats inflation. Additionally, its AFFO payout ratio in 2025 was 75.2%, which is well within the safe region, especially given how defensive its business model is and how strong its balance sheet is. It pays out a current dividend yield of about 5.2%, which, when combined with the dividend growth rate, provides mid-single-digit annualized passive income that should grow at a rate that keeps up with inflation over time.

The Best-of-Breed BDC

In the BDC space, Main Street Capital (MAIN) is about as best of breed as it gets, as it is internally managed, which gives it an operating cost advantage since it does not have to charge shareholders a management fee but instead simply pays its management team compensation, thereby garnering economies of scale. Additionally, its BDC structure requires it to pay out 90% or more of its taxable income, and, given that it primarily invests in debt, it generates a lot of ordinary income that leads to consistently high dividends for shareholders.

Additionally, the fact that MAIN invests a lot of its debt in floating-rate instruments creates nice diversification, as it tends to benefit from rising interest rates, whereas O tends to suffer during rising rates and benefit from falling interest rates. Its five-year return on equity of over 17% is well above peers in the broader financial sector as well, which makes it a very proven and high-quality compounder. Its debt-to-equity ratio of 0.61 times is very conservative for a BDC. Non-accruals of only 1.2% of the portfolio at fair value are one of the lowest in the sector, thereby validating its underwriting skill. MAIN has also grown its dividend for 18 straight years and is therefore only 7 years away from dividend aristocrat status. For a BDC, this is a phenomenal track record.

Combine this with its roughly 6% dividend yield based on just its base dividend, and, actually, it will likely be higher, given that it regularly pays out supplemental dividends on top of it, and MAIN is an attractive income machine that can provide valuable diversification for a portfolio.

Infrastructure Powerhouse With Serious Dividend Growth Potential

Last but not least, Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) is a great pick for getting exposure to infrastructure and the broader real asset sector. It is one of the world's largest alternative asset managers, with $1.2 trillion in assets under management across infrastructure, clean energy (including hydropower and nuclear), wind and solar energy production, private equity, real estate, and credit. It has an asset-light, high-margin, fee-based business in which 80% of its fee-bearing capital is either long-term, permanent, or perpetual in nature. Additionally, it has an A- credit rating from S&P, with $3 billion in corporate liquidity and a 14.5-year weighted average term on its debt. It also has $1.4 billion in either general partner fund co-investments or net accrued carried interest that will provide future liquidity for it as well, with a 4.5% forward-looking dividend yield and a recent 15% dividend increase, as well as strong growth forecasted for the foreseeable future.

BAM combines broad diversification, a strong balance sheet, an attractive dividend yield, and very strong dividend growth potential to give investors an attractive total return outlook. It is worth noting, though, that EPD issues a K-1 tax form, whereas O and MAIN issue 1099s and are U.S.-based, whereas BAM issues a 1099 but is taxed as a Canadian corporation. Investors should keep all these considerations in mind.

It is also worth noting that O and BAM, in particular, are interest-rate-sensitive investments such that when interest rates rise, they tend to experience pressure on their stock price, while MAIN tends to see its income increase when interest rates rise and its income decline when interest rates fall. Finally, EPD, as a midstream infrastructure company, often experiences some volatility with energy prices, though its underlying cash flows are more stable in general than its unit price performance might indicate in the face of energy price volatility.

Why This Portfolio Can Power Your Retirement

Combining these investments alongside SCHD can create a very well-diversified portfolio across a wide spectrum of sectors, with a single-digit income yield and inflation-beating dividend growth over time that should be remarkably consistent across economic environments.

While this is not necessarily a complete portfolio solution, the combination of SCHD, EPD, O, MAIN, and BAM can provide a powerful core for an income investor looking to retire on dividends without having to do much to manage their portfolio. These are exactly the sorts of investments we look for in our retirement portfolio at High Yield Investor, though we do actively manage it and pursue some other alternatives that are even more opportunistically priced at the moment as part of our drive toward generating long-term total return outperformance, a 7–8% dividend yield, and below-market beta.

data.title: Arista Networks: AI Networking Growth With A Durable Moat (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
By Nicholas Tan, Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

In our previous analysis of Arista Networks, Inc.
