(ANET), we highlighted the company’s robust market-share gains in the Ethernet switch market, increasing 9.6% since 2013, in contrast to Cisco, which has seen declines due to a lack of specialization. We further expected the company’s growth outlook to remain robust, driven by its AI partnership with NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) to develop AI-driven networking solutions. We further believed that the company would continue to undergo economies of scale and benefit from low capex spending due to its business model’s focus on selling software solutions and reliance on third-party manufacturing.

In this analysis, we cover the company again, as the company’s revenue climbed 28.60% in 2025 and has grown at an average rate of 27.71% over the last 10 years. We thus analyze whether the company could sustain its revenue growth. We first evaluate whether the company could sustain its competitiveness in the market. Furthermore, we then analyze the market growth opportunity for the company as the Ethernet switch market transitions from 800 Gbps to 1.6 Tbps. Lastly, we evaluate the reasons behind its revenue outperformance against our previous projections and whether the company’s growth outlook remains robust.

Strong Competitive Positioning

We first analyze its competitive positioning in the market and evaluate its market share. We thus compile the Ethernet switch market share of the top competitors from IDC.

We see that its market share has slowly improved from 1.7% in 2013 to 13.0% in Q3 2025 YTD, whereas Cisco’s market share declined from 61.1% in 2013 to 29.3% in Q3 2025 YTD. This highlights that the company has narrowed the gap between itself and the top market leader. The company’s market positioning has also improved from 4th in 2013 to 2nd in Q3 2025, overtaking HPE to become 3rd in 2019 and overtaking Huawei to become 2nd in 2023.

From 2023 to Q3 2025 YTD, Nvidia’s market share increased greatly from 0.5% to 11.6%. This coincided with Nvidia’s 2023 shipping of its Spectrum-4 Ethernet with 64x800 Gbps and 51.2 Tbps of throughput, enabling customers to handle faster data transfers between servers for AI workloads. Nvidia also introduced its Spectrum-X, an Ethernet networking platform, which uses Nvidia’s Spectrum-4 and Nvidia’s BlueField 3 data processing unit (DPU) to improve faster processing of AI workloads.

To further analyze its competitive positioning, we compile its competitors and the company’s latest data center Ethernet switches alongside their specifications. Compared with our previous analysis of Cisco, we added power consumption to better measure the data transfer costs.

We see that its data center Ethernet switches’ performance is relatively in line with its competitors. Its port density of 64x800G and 51.2 Tbps throughput is comparable to Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO), Dell Technologies Inc. (DELL), Nvidia, and Juniper. We also find that Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE) and Huawei have lower port density and weaker throughput compared to the other players’ Ethernet switches. Its power consumption is also lower than that of other competitors with similar port density and throughput, except for Nvidia. We believe this could have contributed to its market positioning, as lower power consumption enables data center companies to reduce operating costs from the high-energy costs of training AI models.

Apart from power consumption, we find that most of the top competitors (Cisco, Arista, Dell, Nvidia, Juniper) have comparable port densities and throughputs. However, the top competitors differ in OS. One unique difference that separates its OS from its competitors is its Extensible Operating System (EOS), whereby the company runs a single software image (software file) across all its products. This enables the company to automate software updates and roll back previous updates with issues across all its products.

By having a single software file running across its products, this lowers operational complexity, improves network reliability, and reduces operational costs for customers, as customers can “automate their IT workflows.” For example, when Arista develops a new software update, customers can deploy the software across its products.

Whereas, when customers use their competitors' products, such as Juniper, HPE, Nvidia, and previously both Cisco and Dell (previous generation), customers need to manage different hardware with different software files alongside quality control testing, which increases the complexity of automation. For example, HPE’s 6300 and 6400 switches use software images named FL.10.xx.yyyy, while HPE’s 6000 and 6100 switches use PL.10.0x.yyyy. This highlights the difference in the software image name. Having a single software image also enables developers to integrate DevOps tools with their products and automate workflows such as coding across all products. This helps developers to “test and deploy network configurations across the entire data center with increased speed and reliability.” Comparatively, running a non-single software file is inefficient, as it slows down software automation and increases complexity.

Facebook’s VP of Infrastructure explained that:

“Arista EOS has proven to be a valuable component of our current designs, providing us with a series of useful features, including better control-plane and data-path programmability, the ability to write traffic steering and monitoring applications that integrate with Sysdb and the entire EOS stack running on our Arista devices, and an SDK framework that is fairly easy to develop and test our code in. All this allows us to have more visibility in and greater control over our network—and that helps us continue to move fast as we scale.”

Arista released its EOS with a single software file running across its products in 2008, whereas Cisco was able to increase its standardization of products within newer product lines, such as the Catalyst 9000 series, beginning in 2018. While Dell also unified some product lines in 2016 with the same software image file. However, one key difference is that Arista was earlier than Cisco and Dell in adopting a single software file, highlighting its early mover advantage to sell its products to customers such as Microsoft and Meta (the earliest confirmation is 2014). In Q4 FY2024, Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) accounted for 20% and 14.6% of 2024 revenue. In Q4 FY2025, we believe that the 2 customers mentioned by the company that accounted for 42% of revenue are likely still Microsoft and Meta.

We can see that for hyperscale customers to migrate from Arista to its competitors’ platforms would require between 12 and 24 months, or an average of 18 months of lost time on retraining and other processes. As such, we believe that one key unique advantage of Arista is its early mover advantage with top hyperscalers, which has enabled the company to “lock in” its hyperscale customers due to the potential time wasted and downtime risk. We thus believe this could explain the reason that Microsoft and Meta remained long-time customers of the company from 2014, despite Cisco offering products that had a single software image.

CloudVision enables data center customers to monitor their network infrastructure in real time, which we believe enhances its competitiveness. Arista has a central state database (SysDB), which holds variables and data, allowing its programs to perform tasks inside the OS. Importantly, by having a central state database, customers can limit software issues to specific programs without impacting other software. Its SysDB also enables software programs to resume their previous tasks if they were unexpectedly terminated, reducing downtime.

We expect the company to sustain its market share in the Ethernet market, driven mainly by its vendor lock-in effect, whereby if hyperscaler customers were to switch Ethernet providers, this would result in downtime risks and time lost. We also believe that the company’s EOS single software image across all its products would remain its unique competitive advantage, enabling the company to be more competitive than its competitors.

While Cisco and Dell currently have a single software image running, the software is only available in some product lines and not across the whole product portfolio. We also believe that its EOS’s capabilities, such as software update automation and DevOps integration for coding automation, as well as its CloudVision and SysDB, would further enhance its software offerings for data center customers and competitiveness. We also expect the company’s hardware products to remain competitive, as the company recently indicated that it is developing 1.6 Tbps this year based on its latest Q4 FY2025 earnings transcript.

Market Growth Opportunity

After analyzing its market positioning and market share, we then analyze whether the data center Ethernet switch market growth could provide any growth opportunities for the company. Ethernet switches are hardware devices that connect network devices via cabling. In relation to AI data centers, training AI workloads, such as model training and inference, requires large amounts of data to be transferred between servers and GPUs to train these models.

AI workloads also require “ultra-high bandwidth, real-time responsiveness, and lossless data transmission.” As such, data center Ethernet switches enable AI data centers to run these workloads by efficiently managing network traffic and transmitting high-speed data among servers, storage, and GPUs, ensuring reliable operation. Transmitting high-speed data for AI workloads is important, as “approximately 20-50% of the job time” is used for communicating data across the network. Thus, as Ethernet port speeds improve and transition from 800 Gbps to 1.6 Tbps, this lowers AI training times and reduces AI bottlenecks.

The data center Ethernet switch market is expected to grow with a 30.8% CAGR from 2024 to 2030F, as the market transitions towards 1.6 Tbps in 2025 and 3.2 Tbps in 2027F. Based on the chart above, the adoption of 1.6 Tbps is expected to drive a large portion of growth between 2026 and 2028F, whereas other Ethernet switches of lower performance are anticipated to have lower or no growth.

Several growth drivers for the market include the AI data center infrastructure buildup (Google’s $40 billion Texas data center and Microsoft Fairwater) to meet AI computing demand, as well as infrastructure upgrades for data centers from 400G and 800G to 1.6 Tbps. AI training also requires higher data throughput, which is also driving the adoption of 1.6 Tbps. In its latest Q4 FY2025 earnings transcript, management also indicated that they are “codesigning several AI rack systems with 1.6T switching emerging this year” and that “1.6 terabit migration is imminent,” which we believe shows that the company is currently designing 1.6 Tbps Ethernet switches. We believe its new product development enables the company to take advantage of the large adoption and market growth from 1.6 Tbps switches beginning in 2027F.

In terms of 3.2 Tbps, management also indicated that they will “look out 18 months to 2 years from now; we're thinking about 800-gig going to 1.6 going to 3.2, and that's where we're kind of looking at the horizon of where we think we're going to be in 2 years,” which we believe highlights that management is planning to develop 3.2 Tbps. Based on the chart, as data center companies start adopting 1.6 Tbps and 3.2 Tbps, the market growth is expected to advance with a CAGR of 30.8%, which we believe the company can take advantage of, as the company is currently developing products for 1.6 Tbps and 3.2 Tbps.

Based on Dell Oro, the data center Ethernet switch market is expected to grow with a CAGR of 30.8%, driven by the increasing adoption of 1.6 Tbps and 3.2 Tbps, which we believe can benefit the company. As AI workloads grow increasingly compute-intensive, this would drive the adoption of faster throughput technologies. We believe that the 30.8% CAGR is reasonable, as we forecast data center capex to grow with a CAGR of 37.8%, which is higher than the 30.8% CAGR and would support the data center Ethernet switch market growth. This is because we believe that the AI infrastructure built out by the top CSPs would support the market growth, as CSPs purchase more of the latest Ethernet switches for faster computing performance.

We also believe that they can take advantage of the transition from 800 Gbps to 1.6 Tbps and 3.2 Tbps, as the company’s Ethernet switch development timeline of 1.6 Tbps this year and 3.2 Tbps in 2028 aligns with the market transition towards higher speeds. As such, we believe the transition towards 1.6 Tbps and 3.2 Tbps would bolster the company’s growth outlook.

Robust Growth Outlook

After determining that the company can take advantage of the market growth, we then analyze why the company outperformed our revenue projections and whether it could sustain its growth outlook. We thus compile their revenue breakdown by business segments and our previous projections to analyze this further. For our previous projections, we previously forecasted the company to grow with the 5-year forward average growth of analysts' consensus and did not forecast individual segments’ growth.

In 2024, we find that the company grew by 19.5%, which is relatively in line with our previous revenue projections of 17.6%. We see that Core grew by $743 million, or 19.5% YoY, whereas the company grew by $1,143 million, highlighting that most of the revenue growth was driven by Core products rather than other segments and has contributed towards higher-than-expected growth.

In 2025, its actual revenue grew by 28.6%, higher than our previous projections of 17.6%. As we indicated above, Microsoft and Meta accounted for 20% and 14.6% of 2024 revenue, totaling 34.6% in 2024. Whereas in 2025, the company reported that 2 customers accounted for 42% of revenue, which we suspect are Microsoft and Meta, as Microsoft and Meta have been long-time customers since 2014, and their total 2025 revenue contribution aligns well with their 2024 contribution. In 2025, both Meta and Microsoft expanded their data center infrastructure for AI model training and to meet cloud computing demands. As such, both Meta and Microsoft have increased their capex by 87.1% and 45.1% for 2025, which resulted in higher-than-expected growth for 2025, as both hyperscalers are customers of Arista.

We overall believe that the company’s growth outlook remains robust, driven by the AI infrastructure buildup, as the company’s Ethernet switches enable hyperscalers such as Meta and Microsoft to efficiently manage their network traffic and transmit high-speed data among their servers, storage, and GPUs. We also believe that its unique competitive advantage, such as its early-mover advantage, enabled the company to lock in top hyperscalers for the long term, due to the large switching costs that could be incurred if hyperscalers swap Ethernet providers.

In terms of forecasting, we forecast its Core and Cognitive Networks segment to grow with our projections of data center capex spending CAGR from 2026 to 2029F. We first calculate the historical data center capex-to-cloud market average growth factor by dividing data center capex growth by cloud market growth. After which, we multiply our growth forecasts for the cloud market against the growth factor to derive data center capex growth for the company. Using the capex growth rate, we then calculate the 2029F data center capex and then calculate the CAGR from 2026F to 2029F. We believe using data center capex spending is reasonable, as its 2 largest customers are Meta and Microsoft, who are hyperscalers driving data center capex spending. For Cognitive Adjacencies, we forecast its growth using its historical average and taper it down to discount potential future uncertainty.

Risk: Higher Memory Prices

We believe one of the key risks that could negatively impact the company is higher memory prices, which would lower its gross margins, as its Ethernet switches use DRAM. As highlighted above, the top 4 hyperscalers are expected to increase their capex by 74.39% in 2026 to train AI models and meet cloud computing demands. AI workloads also require “large amounts of memory,” which increases the demand for memory chips and results in higher memory prices.

In Q1 2026, general-purpose server DRAM prices increased by 98% QoQ and are expected to climb 20% QoQ in Q2 2026, which would negatively impact margins. In its Q4 FY2025 earnings transcript, management indicated that “for gross margin, we reiterate the range for the fiscal year of 62% to 64%, inclusive of mix and anticipated supply chain cost increases for memory and silicon”.

Valuation

Based on our valuation of the company using a DCF model, incorporating our revenue projections at a 5-year forward average growth of around 30.8%, a discount rate of 13.3% reflecting the company’s WACC, and a terminal value based on the Communications Equipment sub-industry average EV/EBITDA multiple of 19.72x, our model indicates an upside of 37.04%.

Verdict

We overall determine that the company’s growth outlook remains robust, driven by AI infrastructure buildout from hyperscale customers such as Microsoft and Meta. We further expect the market transition from 800 Gbps to 1.6 Tbps and 3.2 Tbps to support its growth outlook, as the company has planned 1.6 Tbps and 3.2 Tbps Ethernet switches to benefit from the high market growth. Furthermore, we also expect the company to sustain its strong market positioning in Ethernet due to its early-mover advantage with EOS, which enabled it to sell Ethernet switches to top hyperscalers earlier than competitors, creating a lock-in effect with its hyperscaler customers. We also believe that its EOS with its single software image is a unique competitive advantage, as most of its competitors typically have different types of OS for different hardware.

Based on our DCF valuation, we thus rate Arista Networks as a Strong Buy with a price target of $167.94, which is higher than our previous price target of $83.55 (adjusted for a 4-to-1 stock split), as we forecast the company to grow with a forward 5-year average growth rate of 30.8%, higher than our previous 17.6%. Previously, we calculated its growth projections using the forward 5-year consensus growth average. However, data center capex surged by 53.25% and 63.30% in 2024 and 2025, leading to higher-than-expected growth. And currently, the top 4 hyperscalers’ capex is expected to increase by 74.39% for 2026F. As such, we have readjusted our growth projections higher for the company, as we expect the company to benefit from the higher capex spending.
